era_context: The mid-8th century continued the pattern of post-Umayyad reorganization in the Islamic world, Tang Dynasty recovery from the An Lushan Rebellion, and Frankish consolidation in Western Europe. This was a period of administrative and military restructuring across major Eurasian powers, with limited documentation for most of the world outside these centers.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Frankish realm

events.title: Baghdad construction continues under al-Mansur
events.description: The construction of Baghdad, begun in 762 CE, continued under Caliph al-Mansur's direction. The city was designed as a circular, fortified capital with the caliph's palace at the center, representing a new model of Islamic urbanism and administrative centralization. By 764, the basic infrastructure was taking shape, though the city would not be fully completed until 766 CE. This project symbolized the Abbasid shift from Arab tribal governance to a centralized, Persian-influenced bureaucratic state.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings
al-Khatib al-Baghdadi, History of Baghdad (Tarikh Baghdad)

events.certainty_note: The timeline of Baghdad's construction is well-attested in Islamic sources, though specific progress in 764 CE is not detailed. Archaeological evidence confirms the city's layout and construction phases.

events.title: Byzantine-Arab naval conflict in the Mediterranean
events.region: Eastern Mediterranean (Byzantine Empire vs. Umayyad/Abbasid remnants)
events.description: The Byzantine Empire under Constantine V (r. 741–775 CE) continued its naval campaigns against Arab forces in the Mediterranean. While the major Umayyad Caliphate had fallen in 750 CE, Arab naval power remained a significant threat. Byzantine sources record ongoing raids and counter-raids in the 760s, though specific engagements in 764 CE are not always precisely documented. These conflicts were part of the broader Byzantine-Arab struggle that had defined the 7th and 8th centuries.
events.certainty_note: Byzantine sources document Constantine V's military activities in the 760s, but specific events in 764 CE are not always clearly dated. The broader pattern of Byzantine-Arab conflict is well-attested.

events.title: Iconoclasm controversy in Byzantine Empire
events.description: The Iconoclastic Controversy, which had begun in the early 8th century, continued to dominate Byzantine religious and political life under Constantine V. Constantine V was a strong iconoclast (opponent of religious images), and his policies enforced the destruction of icons and suppression of their veneration. By 764, this had been the official policy for decades, creating significant tension with the monastic establishment and the papacy. The controversy would not be fully resolved until 843 CE.
events.certainty_note: The Iconoclastic Controversy is well-documented in both Byzantine and papal sources. Constantine V's iconoclastic policies are clearly attested for the 760s.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Baghdad's construction is sometimes overstated as an immediate transformation of Islamic governance. In reality, the transition from Umayyad to Abbasid administrative models was gradual, and Baghdad's completion in 766 CE did not immediately resolve factional tensions within the caliphate. Additionally, the Iconoclastic Controversy is often presented as a clear ideological divide, but recent scholarship suggests it was more complex, involving political factionalism, monastic interests, and theological debate rather than a simple binary opposition.
historiographic_note: Sources for 764 CE are concentrated in the Islamic world and Byzantine Empire, where court historiography was well-developed. Islamic sources are often compiled centuries after events and reflect later theological perspectives. Byzantine sources are somewhat more contemporary but still subject to political bias. Western European sources are sparse and often legendary. The absence of documentation for most of the world outside these centers reflects the bias of surviving written records toward literate, centralized states.

era_context: The year 763 CE falls in the immediate aftermath of the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763 CE) in China, the consolidation phase of the Abbasid Caliphate in the Islamic world, and the period of Frankish expansion in Western Europe. This was a transitional year marked by the end of major conflicts and the beginning of new administrative phases in multiple civilizations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Frankish realm

events.title: An Lushan Rebellion ends; Tang Dynasty enters recovery phase
events.description: The An Lushan Rebellion, which had begun in 755 CE, was finally suppressed in 763 CE with the death of the rebel leader Shi Siming and the recapture of Chang'an and Luoyang by Tang forces. This eight-year conflict had devastated northern China, killed an estimated 10–15 million people (though this figure is debated), and fundamentally weakened the Tang Dynasty's military and fiscal capacity. The rebellion marked the end of the Tang's early imperial expansion and the beginning of a long period of decline, though cultural production continued. The recovery phase involved reorganizing the military, restoring administrative control, and dealing with the rise of regional military governors (jiedushi).

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu), compiled by Ouyang Xiu et al., 11th century
Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tang Shu), compiled by Liu Xu et al., 10th century

events.certainty_note: The end of the An Lushan Rebellion in 763 CE is clearly documented in multiple Chinese historical sources. The date is well-attested, though casualty figures are debated by modern scholars.

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate: al-Mansur consolidates power; suppression of Umayyad remnants
events.description: By 763 CE, Caliph al-Mansur had largely consolidated Abbasid power, though pockets of Umayyad resistance and other factional opposition remained. The year 763 saw continued efforts to suppress remaining Umayyad loyalists and establish centralized control over the vast Islamic empire. The Barmakid family, of Persian origin, was rising in prominence as administrators, signaling the shift toward a multi-ethnic, bureaucratic state. This consolidation was not without violence and purges, as al-Mansur eliminated potential rivals and established his authority.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of al-Mansur's consolidation is well-attested, but specific events in 763 CE are not always precisely dated in surviving sources.

events.title: Frankish Kingdom: Pepin III's final campaigns; preparation for succession
events.region: Frankish Kingdom (modern France, Germany, Low Countries)
events.description: Pepin III (r. 751–768 CE) continued his military campaigns in Aquitaine and the Pyrenees in the early 760s. By 763, Pepin was aging and preparing for the succession of his sons, Charlemagne and Carloman. The period 763–768 saw continued consolidation of Frankish power and military campaigns that would provide the foundation for Charlemagne's later expansion. Pepin's reign established the Carolingian dynasty as the dominant military power in Western Europe.
events.certainty_note: The Royal Frankish Annals provide near-contemporary documentation of Frankish military campaigns in the 760s, though specific events in 763 CE are not always detailed.

events.title: Sack of Chang'an by rebel forces during An Lushan Rebellion
events.description: During the final phase of the An Lushan Rebellion, Chang'an, the Tang capital, was sacked and occupied by rebel forces. The city was eventually retaken by Tang loyalist forces under General Guo Ziyi, but the destruction was immense. The sack of Chang'an symbolized the nadir of Tang power and the scale of the rebellion's devastation. The recovery of the capital was a crucial symbolic and strategic victory for the Tang, marking the beginning of the rebellion's end.

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu)

events.certainty_note: The sack of Chang'an during the An Lushan Rebellion is well-documented in Chinese historical sources. The date is clearly attested.

disconfirming_evidence: The casualty figures for the An Lushan Rebellion (often cited as 10–15 million deaths) are debated by modern scholars. Some argue these figures are exaggerated in later sources, while others suggest they may be underestimated. Additionally, the narrative of the rebellion as a simple 'military revolt' obscures its complex origins in regional military autonomy, ethnic tensions, and fiscal crisis. The 'recovery' of the Tang after 763 CE was slower and more incomplete than traditional historiography suggests; the dynasty never fully regained its earlier military dominance.
historiographic_note: Sources for 763 CE are heavily concentrated in Chinese and Islamic historiography. Chinese sources are relatively detailed for the An Lushan Rebellion, though they reflect court perspectives and may underrepresent popular experiences. Islamic sources are less detailed for specific events in 763 CE and are often compiled centuries after the fact. Western European sources are sparse and often legendary. The year 763 CE is significant primarily for events in East Asia and the Islamic world; most of the rest of the world has no documented events for this year.

events.title: Foundation of Baghdad by Caliph al-Mansur
events.description: Caliph al-Mansur (r. 754–775 CE) founded Baghdad as the new capital of the Abbasid Caliphate in 762 CE. The city was designed as a circular, fortified settlement with the caliph's palace at the center, representing a revolutionary model of Islamic urbanism and centralized governance. The site was chosen for its strategic location on the Tigris River, at the intersection of trade routes connecting the Mediterranean, Central Asia, and the Indian Ocean. Construction continued until 766 CE, and Baghdad quickly became one of the largest and most cosmopolitan cities in the world. The founding of Baghdad symbolized the Abbasid shift from Arab tribal governance to a centralized, multi-ethnic, Persian-influenced bureaucratic state.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk)
al-Khatib al-Baghdadi, History of Baghdad (Tarikh Baghdad)

events.certainty_note: The founding of Baghdad in 762 CE is clearly documented in Islamic historical sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence. The date is well-attested.

events.title: Tang Dynasty: Emperor Daizong's accession; continuation of An Lushan Rebellion recovery
events.description: Emperor Daizong of Tang (r. 762–779 CE) ascended to the throne in 762 CE following the death of his father, Emperor Suzong. Daizong inherited a dynasty weakened by the ongoing An Lushan Rebellion (755–763 CE), which was still in its final stages. His reign was marked by efforts to restore central authority, deal with the rise of regional military governors (jiedushi), and manage fiscal crisis caused by the rebellion. Despite these challenges, Daizong's reign saw continued cultural production and the development of new administrative structures.

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu), compiled by Ouyang Xiu et al., 11th century
Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tang Shu), compiled by Liu Xu et al., 10th century

events.certainty_note: The accession of Emperor Daizong in 762 CE is clearly documented in Chinese historical sources. The date is well-attested.

events.title: Frankish Kingdom: Pepin III continues military consolidation
events.region: Frankish Kingdom (modern France, Germany, Low Countries)
events.description: Pepin III (r. 751–768 CE) continued his military campaigns in the early 760s, consolidating Frankish power in Aquitaine and the Pyrenees. By 762, Pepin had established the Carolingian dynasty as the dominant military power in Western Europe. His campaigns against Aquitanian nobles and preparations for the succession of his sons, Charlemagne and Carloman, were ongoing. These military campaigns and administrative consolidations provided the foundation for Charlemagne's later expansion and the creation of the Carolingian Empire.
events.certainty_note: The Royal Frankish Annals provide near-contemporary documentation of Frankish military campaigns in the 760s, though specific events in 762 CE are not always detailed.

events.title: An Lushan Rebellion: Continued fighting in northern China
events.description: The An Lushan Rebellion, which had begun in 755 CE, continued in its seventh year in 762 CE. By this time, the initial rebel leader An Lushan had died (in 757 CE), and his successor Shi Siming was leading the rebellion. The conflict had devastated northern China, disrupted trade routes, and severely weakened the Tang Dynasty's military and fiscal capacity. Fighting continued in the northern regions, with Tang forces gradually gaining ground. The rebellion would finally be suppressed in 763 CE.

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu)

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the An Lushan Rebellion in 762 CE is clearly documented in Chinese historical sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The founding of Baghdad is often presented as an immediate transformation of Islamic governance and culture. However, the transition from Umayyad to Abbasid administrative models was gradual, and Baghdad's completion in 766 CE did not immediately resolve factional tensions within the caliphate or establish it as the undisputed center of Islamic civilization. Additionally, the significance of Baghdad's founding can obscure the continued importance of other Islamic cities like Damascus and the ongoing challenges of consolidating Abbasid power across the vast empire.
historiographic_note: Sources for 762 CE are concentrated in the Islamic world and China, where court historiography was well-developed. Islamic sources for Baghdad's founding are often compiled centuries after the event (al-Tabari wrote in the 9th century, al-Khatib al-Baghdadi in the 11th century) and reflect later theological and political perspectives. Chinese sources are more contemporary but still reflect court perspectives. Western European sources are sparse and often legendary. The year 762 CE is significant primarily for events in the Islamic world and China; most of the rest of the world has no documented events for this year.

events.title: An Lushan Rebellion: Sixth year of conflict; Tang Dynasty under severe strain
events.description: The An Lushan Rebellion, which had begun in 755 CE, continued in its sixth year in 761 CE. By this time, the initial rebel leader An Lushan had died in 757 CE, and his successor Shi Siming was leading the rebellion. The conflict had devastated northern China, disrupted the Grand Canal trade route, and severely weakened the Tang Dynasty's military and fiscal capacity. The rebellion had killed an estimated 10–15 million people (though this figure is debated), making it one of the deadliest conflicts in human history. By 761, the Tang was in severe fiscal crisis, with military campaigns consuming vast resources. The rebellion would finally be suppressed in 763 CE.

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu), compiled by Ouyang Xiu et al., 11th century
Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tang Shu), compiled by Liu Xu et al., 10th century

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the An Lushan Rebellion in 761 CE is clearly documented in Chinese historical sources. The date is well-attested.

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate: al-Mansur consolidates power; administrative reorganization
events.description: Caliph al-Mansur (r. 754–775 CE) continued the consolidation of Abbasid power in 761 CE. By this time, the Abbasid Revolution (750 CE) had been successful in overthrowing the Umayyad Caliphate, but consolidating control over the vast Islamic empire remained a challenge. Al-Mansur was engaged in suppressing remaining Umayyad loyalists, establishing centralized control, and reorganizing the administration along Persian bureaucratic models. The Barmakid family, of Persian origin, was rising in prominence as administrators. This period saw the shift from Arab tribal governance to a centralized, multi-ethnic state. In 762 CE, al-Mansur would found Baghdad as the new capital, symbolizing this transformation.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk)

events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of al-Mansur's consolidation is well-attested in Islamic historiography, though specific events in 761 CE are not always precisely dated in surviving sources.

events.title: Frankish Kingdom: Pepin III's military campaigns continue
events.region: Frankish Kingdom (modern France, Germany, Low Countries)
events.description: Pepin III (r. 751–768 CE) continued his military campaigns in the early 760s, consolidating Frankish power in Aquitaine and the Pyrenees. By 761, Pepin had established the Carolingian dynasty as the dominant military power in Western Europe. His campaigns against Aquitanian nobles and preparations for the succession of his sons, Charlemagne and Carloman, were ongoing. These military campaigns and administrative consolidations provided the foundation for Charlemagne's later expansion and the creation of the Carolingian Empire.
events.certainty_note: The Royal Frankish Annals provide near-contemporary documentation of Frankish military campaigns in the 760s, though specific events in 761 CE are not always detailed.

events.title: Byzantine Empire: Constantine V continues military campaigns
events.description: Emperor Constantine V (r. 741–775 CE) continued his military campaigns against Arab forces in the Mediterranean and against the Bulgars in the Balkans. Constantine V was one of the most militarily active Byzantine emperors, and his reign saw numerous campaigns to defend and expand Byzantine territory. By 761, he had established himself as a formidable military leader, though his reign was also marked by the Iconoclastic Controversy, which created internal religious and political tensions.
events.certainty_note: Byzantine sources document Constantine V's military activities in the 760s, but specific events in 761 CE are not always clearly dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The casualty figures for the An Lushan Rebellion (often cited as 10–15 million deaths) are debated by modern scholars. Some argue these figures are exaggerated in later sources, while others suggest they may be underestimated. Additionally, the narrative of the rebellion as a simple 'military revolt' obscures its complex origins in regional military autonomy, ethnic tensions, and fiscal crisis. The significance of the rebellion in marking the 'decline' of the Tang Dynasty is somewhat overstated; the dynasty continued for another 150 years and remained a major power, though its earlier dominance was diminished.
historiographic_note: Sources for 761 CE are concentrated in China and the Islamic world, where court historiography was well-developed. Chinese sources are relatively detailed for the An Lushan Rebellion, though they reflect court perspectives and may underrepresent popular experiences. Islamic sources are less detailed for specific events in 761 CE and are often compiled centuries after the fact. Byzantine sources are somewhat more contemporary but still subject to political bias. Western European sources are sparse and often legendary. The year 761 CE is significant primarily for events in East Asia, the Islamic world, and the Byzantine Empire; most of the rest of the world has no documented events for this year.

era_context: The mid-8th century CE spans the height of the Abbasid Caliphate's consolidation of power in the Islamic world, the Tang Dynasty's dominance in East Asia, and the Carolingian Empire's expansion in Western Europe. This period marks significant religious, political, and cultural transformations across Eurasia, with the Abbasid Revolution (750 CE) having recently displaced the Umayyads and begun reshaping Islamic governance and intellectual life.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Northern Europe beyond Frankish territories (sparse documentation)
Southeast Asia (limited contemporary chronicles in European archives)

events.title: Abbasid Consolidation and Administrative Reorganization
events.description: The Abbasid Caliphate, having seized power in 750 CE, continues systematic consolidation of authority across the Islamic world. Caliph al-Mansur (r. 754–775 CE) strengthens central administration, establishes the Khorasan Guard as a professional military force, and begins planning the construction of Baghdad as a new capital. This period sees the suppression of remaining Umayyad loyalists and the integration of Persian administrative practices into Islamic governance.
key_figures: Abu Muslim al-Khorasani (military commander, recently deceased 755 CE)

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk)

events.certainty_note: Abbasid administrative reforms are well-attested in later Islamic historiography, though precise dating of specific initiatives within the 750s–760s is sometimes uncertain. The general trajectory is secure.

events.title: Tang Dynasty Military Campaigns in Central Asia
events.description: The Tang Dynasty continues military operations to maintain control over the Silk Road and Central Asian territories. Following the Battle of the Talas River (751 CE), where Tang forces were defeated by the Abbasid Caliphate and Central Asian allies, Tang military presence in the region remains contested. By 760 CE, Tang forces are consolidating positions in the Tarim Basin and managing relationships with Turkic and Tibetan powers.
key_figures: An Lushan (military governor, recently deceased 757 CE)

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu), compiled 11th century
Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji) and continuations

events.certainty_note: Tang military activities in Central Asia are documented in Chinese chronicles, though specific campaigns in 760 CE are sometimes conflated with broader regional conflicts. The general pattern of Tang retrenchment after Talas is secure.

events.title: Frankish Expansion under Pepin III and Early Charlemagne
events.description: Pepin III (r. 751–768 CE) continues consolidation of Frankish power in Western Europe. His son Charles (later Charlemagne) is being groomed for succession and participates in military campaigns. The Frankish kingdom expands its influence over neighboring territories and strengthens ties with the Papacy, establishing the foundation for what will become the Carolingian Empire.

sources.name: Einhard, Life of Charlemagne (Vita Karoli Magni)

events.certainty_note: Frankish political developments are documented in Carolingian-era chronicles, though these were compiled after the events. The general trajectory of Pepin's reign and Charlemagne's early life is well-established.
cross_references: 768-001 (Pepin III's death and division of kingdom)

events.title: Byzantine-Arab Naval Conflicts in the Mediterranean
events.description: The Byzantine Empire under Constantine V (r. 741–775 CE) continues naval warfare against Arab forces in the Mediterranean. Byzantine fleets engage in raids and defensive operations against Umayyad and early Abbasid naval power. These conflicts are part of the broader struggle for control of Mediterranean trade routes and coastal territories.
key_figures: Arab naval commanders (names often unrecorded in Byzantine sources)
events.certainty_note: Byzantine-Arab naval conflicts are attested in Byzantine chronicles, but specific engagements in 760 CE are often undated or conflated with broader campaigns. The general pattern of ongoing Mediterranean conflict is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of specific events in 760 CE is difficult to assess because many sources conflate multiple years of the 750s–760s period. The Battle of Talas River (751 CE) is sometimes dated to 751 or 752 CE with scholarly disagreement. An Lushan's death is firmly dated to 757 CE, but the aftermath and Tang military reorganization in 760 CE are less precisely documented. No major disconfirming evidence contradicts the events listed, but their individual significance relative to broader trends is uncertain.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 760 CE is moderate but unevenly distributed. Islamic sources (later compilations like Tabari) provide detailed accounts of Abbasid administrative developments, though these were written centuries later and may reflect later institutional practices. Chinese chronicles offer good coverage of Tang military and political affairs, though specific campaigns are sometimes undated. Byzantine and Frankish sources are sparser for this year specifically, though the broader era is well-documented. The record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military history; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and Northern Europe are largely absent from surviving written sources.

events.title: An Lushan Rebellion Continues in Tang China
events.description: The An Lushan Rebellion, which began in 755 CE, continues to devastate Tang territories. An Lushan himself died in 757 CE, but his son An Qingxu continues the rebellion with support from Uyghur allies. The Tang Dynasty, under Emperor Suzong (r. 756–762 CE), struggles to suppress the rebellion and maintain control over northern China. This conflict causes massive civilian casualties, economic disruption, and territorial fragmentation.
key_figures: An Qingxu (son of An Lushan, rebel leader)

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu)
Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tang Shu)

events.certainty_note: The An Lushan Rebellion is extensively documented in Chinese chronicles. The continuation of the rebellion in 759 CE is well-attested, though specific military engagements may be undated.

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate Suppresses Umayyad Remnants
events.description: The Abbasid Caliphate under al-Mansur continues to eliminate remaining Umayyad loyalists and consolidate central authority. This period sees the integration of Persian administrative practices and the strengthening of the Khorasan Guard. The Abbasid state is establishing itself as a more centralized, bureaucratic empire compared to the decentralized Umayyad system.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Abbasid consolidation is well-documented in later Islamic historiography, but specific events in 759 CE are often undated or conflated with broader trends of the 750s–760s.

events.title: Frankish Military Campaigns under Pepin III
events.description: Pepin III continues military campaigns to expand and consolidate Frankish power. Campaigns against Aquitaine, Saxony, and other neighboring territories are conducted to extend Frankish hegemony. These military operations establish the foundation for Charlemagne's later expansionist campaigns.
events.certainty_note: Frankish military campaigns are documented in Carolingian-era chronicles, but specific campaigns in 759 CE are often undated or conflated with broader patterns of expansion.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the specific significance of 759 CE as a distinct year is difficult to establish because many sources treat the 755–763 CE period (the An Lushan Rebellion) as a continuous conflict without precise annual dating. The Abbasid consolidation is documented, but 759 CE is not marked as a particularly significant year in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 759 CE is sparse. Chinese chronicles provide the most detailed coverage, focusing on the An Lushan Rebellion. Islamic sources (later compilations) offer general accounts of Abbasid consolidation but rarely date specific events to individual years. Frankish sources are even sparser for this year. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political history; economic, social, and cultural developments are largely absent. The year 759 CE does not appear to be marked as particularly significant in any surviving historical tradition, suggesting that major events may have been undated or that this year was relatively quiet in terms of recorded history.

events.title: Death of An Lushan; Rebellion Continues under An Qingxu
events.description: An Lushan, the Tang military governor whose rebellion began in 755 CE, dies in 757 CE (some sources date this to late 756 CE). His son An Qingxu assumes leadership of the rebellion, which continues to devastate Tang territories. The death of An Lushan does not end the rebellion but marks a transition in its leadership. The Tang Dynasty, under Emperor Suzong, continues to struggle against the rebellion with the aid of Uyghur allies.

events.title: An Lushan Rebellion Reaches Peak; Tang Dynasty in Crisis
events.description: The An Lushan Rebellion, which began in 755 CE, reaches its peak in 756 CE. An Lushan's forces capture the Tang capital of Chang'an (modern Xi'an) and establish a rival state. Emperor Xuanzong flees to Sichuan, and his son Suzong assumes the throne. The rebellion causes massive civilian casualties, economic disruption, and territorial fragmentation. The Tang Dynasty faces an existential crisis as rebel forces control significant portions of northern China.

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu)
Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tang Shu)
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The capture of Chang'an and the succession of Suzong are firmly dated to 756 CE in Chinese chronicles. This is one of the most significant events of the year.

The Abbasid state is establishing itself as a more centralized, bureaucratic empire compared to the decentralized Umayyad system.

Pepin's military campaigns against Aquitaine, Saxony, and other regions extend Frankish hegemony.

events.title: Umayyad Survivor Abd al-Rahman I Establishes Emirate in Iberia
events.description: Abd al-Rahman I, a survivor of the Umayyad dynasty, establishes the Umayyad Emirate of Córdoba in Iberia (Al-Andalus) after fleeing the Abbasid Revolution. This marks the beginning of a new Umayyad state independent of the Abbasid Caliphate. The establishment of this emirate creates a significant Islamic power center in Western Europe and establishes a rival to Abbasid authority.
key_figures: Abd al-Rahman I (founder of Umayyad Emirate of Córdoba)

The date 756 CE is the traditional date for the founding, though some sources place it slightly earlier or later. The general event is confirmed, but precise dating may vary by ±1–2 years.

The traditional date is 756 CE, but scholarly consensus allows for ±1–2 years of uncertainty. No other major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed.

Islamic sources document the establishment of the Umayyad Emirate in Iberia, though dating varies slightly. Frankish and Byzantine sources are sparser for this year. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political history; economic, social, and cultural developments are largely absent. The year 756 CE is marked as significant in Chinese and Islamic historiography but less so in Western European sources.

This period marks major geopolitical realignments: the Abbasids have recently overthrown the Umayyads (750 CE), the An Lushan Rebellion is destabilizing Tang China, and the Frankish kingdom under Pepin III is establishing itself as a major European power. Byzantine Empire faces external pressures from Arab and Bulgar forces.

events.title: An Lushan Rebellion begins
events.region: Tang Dynasty, China (Hebei and Henan provinces)
events.description: An Lushan, a Sogdian-Turkic general in the Tang military, declares himself emperor and launches a major rebellion against the Tang Dynasty. This marks the beginning of one of the most destructive civil wars in Chinese history, lasting until 763 CE. The rebellion will kill millions, destabilize the Tang state, and fundamentally weaken central authority, leading to the rise of regional military governors (jiedushi) and the eventual fragmentation of Tang power. The event reshapes East Asian geopolitics for generations.

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang), compiled 945 CE
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang), compiled 1060 CE
Contemporary Tang administrative records and memorials

events.certainty_note: The rebellion's outbreak in 755 CE is firmly established in multiple Tang historical records and later compilations. Dating is precise.
cross_references: Follows from 750-001 (Abbasid overthrow of Umayyads, contemporary regional instability)

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate consolidates control in Iraq and Persia
events.description: Five years after overthrowing the Umayyad Caliphate (750 CE), the Abbasid dynasty under Caliph al-Mansur (r. 754–775) continues consolidating administrative and military control over the Islamic heartland. This period sees the establishment of a centralized bureaucracy, the recruitment of Persian administrative talent, and the suppression of remaining Umayyad loyalists and other rival factions. The Abbasids are laying the groundwork for the construction of Baghdad (founded 762 CE) as their new capital.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk), compiled early 10th century

events.certainty_note: The Abbasid consolidation is well-documented in Islamic historiography and numismatic evidence. The year 755 CE marks al-Mansur's accession and the execution of Abu Muslim, a pivotal moment.
cross_references: Part of broader Abbasid rise (750–800 CE)

events.title: Pepin III's Frankish kingdom expands in Italy
events.description: Pepin III (the Short), king of the Franks, campaigns in Italy to support Pope Stephen II against Lombard encroachment. These campaigns, which began in 754 CE, continue through 755 and result in Frankish military victories that secure Papal territorial interests. The Frankish intervention establishes a crucial alliance between the Frankish monarchy and the Papacy, laying the foundation for the later Carolingian Empire and the concept of Papal temporal power backed by Frankish military might.

sources.name: Liber Pontificalis (Book of the Popes), compiled 8th–9th centuries
Annales Regni Francorum (Annals of the Frankish Kingdom)
Paul the Deacon, History of the Lombards (Historia Langobardorum), compiled late 8th century

events.certainty_note: The campaigns are attested in Frankish and Papal sources, though exact chronology of individual years (754–756) shows minor variation across sources. 755 CE is a probable date for continued operations.
cross_references: Leads to 756-001 (Donation of Pepin, territorial settlement)

events.title: Byzantine-Bulgarian tensions and frontier warfare
events.description: The Byzantine Empire under Constantine V continues military operations against the First Bulgarian Empire under Khan Krum's predecessors. Frontier raids and skirmishes characterize this period, as both powers contest control of the Balkans. These conflicts are part of a longer pattern of Byzantine-Bulgarian rivalry that will intensify in the 9th century. The exact events of 755 CE are poorly documented, but the broader military context is clear from later chronicles.
key_figures: Bulgarian khans (identity uncertain for this specific year)

sources.name: Theophanes the Confessor, Chronicle (Chronographia), compiled early 9th century

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Byzantine-Bulgarian conflict is confirmed, but specific events in 755 CE are not clearly dated in surviving sources. This entry reflects the era context rather than a precisely documented event.
cross_references: Part of longer Byzantine-Bulgarian conflict (7th–11th centuries)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the An Lushan Rebellion's outbreak in 755 CE is universally accepted, but modern scholarship has questioned earlier narratives that portrayed An Lushan as a purely external threat. Recent work emphasizes that An Lushan was deeply embedded in Tang military and political structures, and the rebellion reflected internal Tang factional conflicts as much as external invasion. Additionally, some scholars debate whether the rebellion's death toll (traditionally cited as 10–15 million) is exaggerated in later sources. The Frankish-Papal alliance is sometimes overstated in Western historiography as a deliberate 'donation' when it was more pragmatic military cooperation; the formal 'Donation of Pepin' is a later construct.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 755 CE is heavily skewed toward East Asia (Tang China) and the Islamic world, where contemporary administrative records and near-contemporary chronicles survive. Western European sources are sparse and often compiled centuries later. Byzantine sources are fragmentary. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are essentially absent from the historical record for this year. The surviving sources are also shaped by court-sponsored historiography (Tang, Abbasid, Frankish) and religious institutions (Papal, Byzantine), which may emphasize political legitimacy and downplay internal instability or military failures.

This is a pivotal moment of transition across multiple civilizations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and Indian subcontinent (limited contemporary records)

events.title: Pepin III's first Italian campaign against the Lombards
events.description: Pepin III (the Short), king of the Franks, launches his first military campaign into Italy at the request of Pope Stephen II to counter Lombard King Aistulf's territorial expansion and threats to Papal lands. The campaign is successful, with Frankish forces defeating the Lombards and securing Papal interests. This intervention marks the beginning of a crucial military and political alliance between the Frankish monarchy and the Papacy, fundamentally reshaping Western European politics and establishing the Franks as the dominant military power in the region.
key_figures: Pepin III (King of the Franks, r. 751–768)

sources.name: Liber Pontificalis (Book of the Popes), compiled 8th–9th centuries
Annales Regni Francorum (Annals of the Frankish Kingdom), compiled early 9th century
Paul the Deacon, History of the Lombards, compiled late 8th century

events.certainty_note: The campaign is well-attested in Frankish and Papal sources. The date of 754 CE is consistent across major chronicles, though some sources conflate the first and second campaigns (754 and 756 CE).
cross_references: Leads to 756-001 (Donation of Pepin and territorial settlement)

events.title: Accession of Caliph al-Mansur to the Abbasid throne
events.description: Abu Ja'far al-Mansur (r. 754–775) becomes the second Abbasid Caliph following the death of his brother al-Saffah. Al-Mansur is a transformative figure who consolidates Abbasid power, establishes a centralized bureaucracy, and begins the construction of Baghdad as the new capital (762 CE). His reign marks the transition from the revolutionary phase of the Abbasid takeover to a stable, institutionalized caliphate. Al-Mansur's policies emphasize Persian administrative models and reduce the influence of Arab tribal elites.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, compiled early 10th century

events.certainty_note: Al-Mansur's accession in 754 CE is firmly established in Islamic historiography and numismatic evidence. The date is precise.

events.title: Council of Hieria and Iconoclastic synod in Byzantine Empire
events.description: The Council of Hieria (also called the Synod of 754) is convened by Byzantine Emperor Constantine V to address the veneration of religious images. The council condemns the use of icons in religious worship, reinforcing the Iconoclastic position that had been official policy since the early 8th century. This council represents the height of Byzantine Iconoclasm and is later condemned by the Second Council of Nicaea (787 CE). The council's decisions have profound theological and political implications, affecting religious practice across the Byzantine Empire and influencing relations with the Western Church.

sources.name: Theophanes the Confessor, Chronicle, compiled early 9th century
Acts of the Council of Hieria (fragmentary, preserved in later sources)
Second Council of Nicaea (787 CE), which references and condemns the Council of Hieria

events.certainty_note: The Council of Hieria is firmly dated to 754 CE in Byzantine sources. However, the original acts of the council are lost; our knowledge comes from later sources, particularly the Second Council of Nicaea's refutation.
cross_references: Part of broader Iconoclastic period (726–843 CE)
Leads to 787-001 (Second Council of Nicaea, reversal of Iconoclasm)

events.title: Tang Dynasty administrative reforms and military reorganization
events.description: Emperor Xuanzong of Tang continues administrative reforms and military reorganization, though the empire is beginning to show signs of internal strain. The military system is being restructured, with increasing reliance on non-Han military commanders (like An Lushan) in frontier positions. These reforms are intended to strengthen central authority, but they inadvertently create the conditions for the An Lushan Rebellion (755 CE) by concentrating military power in the hands of ambitious regional commanders. The year 754 CE represents the calm before the storm of the rebellion.

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang), compiled 945 CE
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang), compiled 1060 CE

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Tang administrative reforms is well-documented, but specific events in 754 CE are not precisely dated in surviving sources. This entry reflects the era context and the trajectory leading to the An Lushan Rebellion.

disconfirming_evidence: The Council of Hieria is sometimes presented as a major theological innovation, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it was largely a formalization of existing Iconoclastic policy under Constantine V. The council's acts were later destroyed or heavily edited, so our understanding of its precise theological positions is limited and mediated through hostile sources (the Second Council of Nicaea). The Frankish-Papal alliance is sometimes portrayed as a deliberate strategic partnership, but it was more pragmatic and transactional; Pepin III's motivations included territorial expansion and legitimacy-building, not purely religious devotion. The Tang administrative reforms are often blamed for creating the conditions for the An Lushan Rebellion, but recent scholarship suggests the rebellion was more a product of broader geopolitical instability and factional conflicts within the Tang elite.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 754 CE is strongest for the Islamic world (Abbasid accession), Western Europe (Frankish-Papal relations), and the Byzantine Empire (Council of Hieria). East Asian sources are less precise about specific events in this year, though the broader Tang context is clear. The surviving sources are heavily shaped by institutional interests: Papal sources emphasize Frankish military support, Frankish sources emphasize their role as defenders of the Church, and Byzantine sources emphasize imperial religious authority. Islamic sources are more focused on administrative consolidation than military events. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are absent from the historical record.

The Byzantine Empire remains engaged in Iconoclasm and frontier conflicts. This year falls in a period of significant geopolitical realignment across Eurasia, with major powers consolidating or facing internal challenges.

events.title: Abbasid consolidation of power in Persia and Iraq
events.description: Under Caliph al-Mansur (r. 754–775, but with significant influence before his formal accession), the Abbasid Caliphate continues consolidating control over the Islamic heartland. The year 753 CE falls in the final year of al-Saffah's reign and the transition to al-Mansur's rule. This period sees the suppression of remaining Umayyad loyalists, the establishment of a centralized bureaucracy, and the recruitment of Persian administrative talent. The Abbasids are laying the groundwork for their new capital at Baghdad, which will be founded in 762 CE.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, compiled early 10th century

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Abbasid consolidation is confirmed, but specific events in 753 CE are not precisely documented. This entry reflects the era context and the trajectory of Abbasid development.

events.title: Tang Dynasty military and administrative developments
events.description: Emperor Xuanzong of Tang continues to govern during a period of relative stability, though underlying tensions are building. The military system is being restructured with increasing reliance on non-Han commanders in frontier positions. The empire's administrative apparatus remains strong, but factional conflicts within the court are intensifying. The year 753 CE represents the final years of relative peace before the An Lushan Rebellion (755 CE) destabilizes the empire.

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang), compiled 945 CE
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang), compiled 1060 CE

events.certainty_note: Specific events in 753 CE are not clearly documented in surviving sources. This entry reflects the broader era context and the trajectory leading to the An Lushan Rebellion.

events.title: Byzantine-Bulgarian frontier conflicts
events.description: The Byzantine Empire under Constantine V continues military operations against the First Bulgarian Empire. Frontier raids and skirmishes characterize this period, as both powers contest control of the Balkans. These conflicts are part of a longer pattern of Byzantine-Bulgarian rivalry that will intensify in the 9th century. The exact events of 753 CE are poorly documented, but the broader military context is clear from later chronicles.

sources.name: Theophanes the Confessor, Chronicle, compiled early 9th century

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Byzantine-Bulgarian conflict is confirmed, but specific events in 753 CE are not clearly dated in surviving sources.
cross_references: Part of longer Byzantine-Bulgarian conflict (7th–11th centuries)

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the sparse documentation for 753 CE means that any events attributed to this year are largely inferred from broader era context rather than specific contemporary sources. The significance of this year is primarily as a transition point between al-Saffah and al-Mansur in the Abbasid Caliphate, and as the final year of relative peace in the Tang Dynasty before the An Lushan Rebellion.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 753 CE is extremely sparse. Most surviving sources are later compilations (9th–11th centuries) that do not provide precise year-by-year detail for this period. The historical record is heavily skewed toward major political transitions (Abbasid succession) and long-term trends (Tang military reorganization, Byzantine-Bulgarian conflicts) rather than specific events. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are absent from the historical record. The surviving sources are shaped by institutional interests and may omit or downplay events that do not fit the narrative of imperial consolidation or religious authority.

events.title: Pope Stephen II's accession and appeal to the Franks
events.region: Papal States and Frankish Kingdom (Italy and Western Europe)
events.description: Pope Stephen II (r. 752–757) becomes pope and faces immediate threats from Lombard King Aistulf, who is expanding Lombard territory at the expense of Papal lands. Stephen II seeks military assistance from the Frankish kingdom under Pepin III (the Short), initiating diplomatic negotiations that will lead to the Frankish-Papal alliance and Pepin's Italian campaigns (754–756 CE). This moment marks the beginning of a crucial political and military partnership that will reshape Western European politics and establish the Franks as the dominant military power in the region.
key_figures: Pepin III (King of the Franks, r. 751–768)

sources.name: Liber Pontificalis (Book of the Popes), compiled 8th–9th centuries
Annales Regni Francorum (Annals of the Frankish Kingdom), compiled early 9th century

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate consolidation under al-Saffah
events.description: Caliph al-Saffah (r. 750–754) continues consolidating Abbasid power in the Islamic heartland. The year 752 CE falls in the middle of al-Saffah's reign, a period marked by the suppression of remaining Umayyad loyalists, the establishment of a centralized bureaucracy, and the recruitment of Persian administrative talent. Al-Saffah's reign is relatively brief (750–754 CE), but it establishes the foundations for the more transformative reign of his successor, al-Mansur (754–775 CE).

The year 752 CE represents the final years of relative peace before the An Lushan Rebellion (755 CE) destabilizes the empire. Underlying tensions are building, but they are not yet visible in the historical record.

era_context: The mid-8th century CE is marked by major geopolitical realignments: the Abbasid Caliphate has recently overthrown the Umayyads (750 CE) and is consolidating power, the Tang Dynasty is approaching internal crisis, and the Frankish kingdom under Pepin III (the Short) is establishing itself as a major European power. The Byzantine Empire remains engaged in Iconoclasm and frontier conflicts. This year falls in a period of significant transition across multiple civilizations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and Indian subcontinent (limited contemporary records)

events.title: Battle of the Talas River
events.description: The Battle of the Talas River is fought between the Abbasid Caliphate (allied with local Central Asian powers) and the Tang Dynasty (allied with the Karakhanid Khanate and other Central Asian allies). The battle is a major military engagement that results in an Abbasid-allied victory and marks a significant shift in the balance of power in Central Asia. The Tang Dynasty's influence in Central Asia is severely diminished, while the Abbasid Caliphate's power and prestige are enhanced. The battle has long-term consequences for the Silk Road trade routes and the geopolitical balance between the Islamic world and East Asia. The exact date of the battle is debated by scholars, with some sources placing it in 751 CE and others in 752 CE.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, compiled early 10th century
Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang), compiled 945 CE
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang), compiled 1060 CE

events.certainty_note: The Battle of the Talas River is attested in both Islamic and Chinese sources, but the exact date is debated. Most modern scholars place it in 751 CE, but some sources suggest 752 CE. The battle's significance is undisputed, but the precise chronology is uncertain.
cross_references: Part of broader Tang-Abbasid rivalry in Central Asia (7th–8th centuries)
Leads to 755-001 (An Lushan Rebellion, which further weakens Tang power in Central Asia)

events.title: Pepin III's consolidation of Frankish power
events.description: Pepin III (the Short), king of the Franks (r. 751–768), consolidates his power and establishes the Carolingian dynasty. Pepin III has recently deposed the last Merovingian king (Childeric III) and established himself as the legitimate ruler of the Frankish kingdom. The year 751 CE marks the beginning of Pepin III's reign and the establishment of a new dynasty that will dominate Western Europe for the next century. Pepin III's reign is characterized by military expansion, administrative reform, and the cultivation of an alliance with the Papacy.
key_figures: Pepin III (King of the Franks, r. 751–768)

sources.name: Annales Regni Francorum (Annals of the Frankish Kingdom), compiled early 9th century
Liber Pontificalis (Book of the Popes), compiled 8th–9th centuries
Paul the Deacon, History of the Lombards, compiled late 8th century

Leads to 754-001 (Pepin III's first Italian campaign)

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate consolidation in the year following the revolution
events.description: The Abbasid Caliphate, having recently overthrown the Umayyad dynasty (750 CE), continues consolidating power under Caliph al-Saffah (r. 750–754). The year 751 CE falls in the early phase of Abbasid consolidation, marked by the suppression of remaining Umayyad loyalists, the establishment of a centralized bureaucracy, and the recruitment of Persian administrative talent. The Abbasids are laying the groundwork for their new capital at Baghdad, which will be founded in 762 CE.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, compiled early 10th century

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Abbasid consolidation is confirmed, but specific events in 751 CE are not precisely documented.

events.title: Tang Dynasty military operations in Central Asia
events.description: The Tang Dynasty, under Emperor Xuanzong, maintains military operations in Central Asia. The year 751 CE is marked by the Battle of the Talas River, which represents a major setback for Tang military power in the region. The battle's outcome shifts the balance of power in Central Asia away from the Tang Dynasty and toward the Abbasid Caliphate. This military defeat has long-term consequences for Tang influence in Central Asia and contributes to the empire's later internal instability.

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang), compiled 945 CE
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang), compiled 1060 CE

events.certainty_note: The Battle of the Talas River is attested in both Islamic and Chinese sources, but the exact date is debated. Most modern scholars place it in 751 CE, but some sources suggest 752 CE.
cross_references: Related to 751-001 (Battle of the Talas River)
Leads to 755-001 (An Lushan Rebellion, which further weakens Tang power)

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of the Talas River is sometimes presented as a decisive turning point in the geopolitical balance between the Islamic world and East Asia, but modern scholarship has questioned the extent of its immediate impact. Some scholars argue that the battle's significance has been exaggerated in later sources, and that Tang influence in Central Asia declined gradually rather than suddenly. Additionally, the exact date of the battle is disputed: while most modern scholars place it in 751 CE, some sources (particularly Chinese chronicles) suggest 752 CE. The battle's casualty figures, often cited as extremely high (tens of thousands), are likely exaggerated in later sources. Pepin III's deposition of the last Merovingian king is sometimes presented as a revolutionary moment, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the Merovingian dynasty had already lost effective power; Pepin III's accession was more a formalization of existing power relationships than a dramatic overthrow.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 751 CE is sparse but somewhat richer than surrounding years due to the significance of the Battle of the Talas River and Pepin III's accession. The Battle of the Talas River is attested in both Islamic and Chinese sources, though the original accounts are lost and our knowledge comes from later compilations (9th–11th centuries). The exact date is debated, and the battle's immediate impact may have been overstated in later sources. Pepin III's accession is well-documented in Frankish and Papal sources. The Abbasid consolidation is less precisely documented for this specific year. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are absent from the historical record. The surviving sources are shaped by institutional interests (Islamic, Frankish, Papal, Chinese) and may emphasize events that fit the narrative of institutional consolidation or military triumph.

era_context: The mid-8th century CE spans the early Abbasid Caliphate (established 750), the Tang Dynasty at its height in China, the Carolingian Renaissance under Pepin III and early Charlemagne in Western Europe, and the consolidation of Islamic rule across the Mediterranean and Central Asia. This is a pivotal moment of religious, political, and cultural transformation across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Oceania and Pacific islands (oral traditions not well-preserved in written form)
Northern Europe beyond Frankish territories (sparse contemporary documentation)

events.title: Battle of the Great Zab and fall of the Umayyad Caliphate
events.description: On 25 July 750 CE, Abbasid forces under Abu Muslim Khorasani defeated the last Umayyad caliph Marwan II at the Great Zab River. This decisive victory ended nearly a century of Umayyad rule (661–750) and inaugurated the Abbasid Caliphate, which would dominate the Islamic world for centuries. The battle marked a shift in the centre of Islamic power from Syria to Iraq and signalled the rise of Persian administrative influence within the caliphate. Marwan II fled to Egypt, where he was hunted down and killed in 750 or 751.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk)

events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in multiple Islamic and Byzantine sources, though written down generations later. The date of 25 July 750 is given by al-Tabari and is accepted by modern scholarship. Marwan II's death is sometimes dated to 750 or 751; sources differ slightly.
cross_references: Relates to 751-001 (Battle of Talas, Abbasid expansion eastward)

events.title: Establishment of the Abbasid Caliphate
events.description: Following the victory at the Great Zab, Abu al-Abbas as-Saffah was proclaimed the first Abbasid caliph in Khorasan in 750 CE. The Abbasid revolution, which had been building for decades through underground networks (the Hashimiyya), now became the dominant Islamic state. The Abbasids promised a more inclusive, merit-based administration and claimed descent from Muhammad's uncle al-Abbas. This marked a fundamental shift from Umayyad Arab aristocracy to a more cosmopolitan, Persian-influenced bureaucratic model that would define Islamic governance for centuries.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings
Al-Jahshiyari, Book of Ministers and Secretaries

events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Abbasid Caliphate in 750 is well-attested in Islamic historiography. The date is secure, though details of early administrative structure are reconstructed from later sources.

events.title: Tang Dynasty at peak territorial extent under Xuanzong
events.description: In 750 CE, the Tang Dynasty under Emperor Xuanzong (r. 712–756) was at or near its maximum territorial extent, controlling vast regions from Korea to Central Asia. The Tang had recently consolidated control over the Tarim Basin and maintained military garrisons along the Silk Road. However, this year marks the beginning of the end of Tang dominance: the An Lushan Rebellion would erupt in 755, and Xuanzong's reign would be remembered as the transition from Tang expansion to contraction. The court at Chang'an was a centre of cosmopolitan culture, attracting merchants, monks, and scholars from across Asia.
key_figures: An Lushan (general, not yet in rebellion)
Yang Guifei (imperial concubine, influential at court)

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang), compiled 945 CE
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang), compiled 1060 CE

events.certainty_note: The Tang Dynasty's territorial extent and Xuanzong's reign are well-documented in Chinese historical records. The year 750 is a conventional marker; the transition from expansion to contraction was gradual.

events.title: Pepin III consolidates Frankish power; Donation of Pepin
events.region: Frankish kingdom (modern France, parts of Germany and Italy)
events.description: In 750 CE, Pepin III (the Short), mayor of the palace under the last Merovingian king Childeric III, moved to legitimise his rule. In 751, Pepin deposed Childeric and was crowned king by Pope Zachary, establishing the Carolingian dynasty. The events of 750–751 represent a crucial transition in Western European politics: the shift from Merovingian to Carolingian rule and the formalisation of the Frankish-papal alliance. Pepin's military campaigns against the Lombards and his subsequent 'Donation of Pepin' (756) would lay the foundation for the Papal States and the later Carolingian Empire.
key_figures: Pepin III (the Short, mayor of the palace, soon to be king)

sources.name: Einhard, Life of Charlemagne (Vita Karoli Magni)

events.certainty_note: The deposition of Childeric III and Pepin's rise are attested in multiple sources, but the exact date is sometimes given as 751 rather than 750. The sources are not contemporary; the Liber Pontificalis was compiled in the 9th century.
cross_references: Leads to 756-001 (Donation of Pepin to the papacy)

events.title: Iconoclasm in the Byzantine Empire under Constantine V
events.description: In 750 CE, the Byzantine Emperor Constantine V (r. 741–775) was actively enforcing iconoclasm—the destruction and prohibition of religious images. Constantine V had inherited the iconoclastic policy from his father Leo III and pursued it with vigour, convening the Council of Hieria in 754 to condemn icon veneration as idolatry. This period of intense iconoclasm (726–787) caused significant religious and social upheaval, leading to the destruction of countless religious artworks and the persecution of icon-venerating monks. The policy was deeply unpopular with the populace and the papacy, contributing to the growing estrangement between Eastern and Western Christianity.
key_figures: Leo III (Constantine's father, initiator of iconoclasm)

sources.name: John of Damascus, On the Divine Images (Apologia contra eos qui aboleverunt imagines)

events.certainty_note: Iconoclasm under Constantine V is well-attested in Byzantine and Western sources. The Council of Hieria is dated to 754, and Constantine's policies in 750 are documented in later chronicles.
cross_references: Relates to 787-001 (Second Council of Nicaea, restoration of icons)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 750 CE as a 'turning point' is somewhat historiographically constructed. While the Battle of the Great Zab and the fall of the Umayyads are confirmed events, the notion that the Abbasid Caliphate immediately represented a fundamental improvement in governance or inclusivity is disputed by modern scholars. Some argue that the Abbasid revolution was primarily a power transfer among elites, with limited immediate benefit to non-Arab Muslims. Additionally, the dating of Pepin III's deposition of Childeric III is sometimes placed in 751 rather than 750, and the 'Donation of Pepin' is a later papal construction; the actual extent of Pepin's territorial grants to the papacy is debated. Constantine V's iconoclasm is confirmed, but its characterisation as uniquely destructive or unpopular is partly shaped by later pro-icon sources written by his opponents.
historiographic_note: The year 750 CE is well-documented in Islamic, Chinese, and Western European sources, though most surviving accounts were written generations or centuries later. Islamic historiography is dominated by al-Tabari and later compilers, who drew on earlier (now lost) sources; this creates a risk of narrative distortion. Chinese sources are abundant but court-sponsored and may downplay internal instability. Western European sources are sparse and often hagiographic. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Indian subcontinent, and Oceania are largely absent from contemporary documentation for this year. The 'significance' of 750 is partly a retrospective construction: the Abbasid victory was momentous, but its long-term consequences were not immediately apparent.

Tang territorial extent and Xuanzong's reign set the stage for the An Lushan Rebellion
